input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin described relies on Model‑Based Systems‑Engineering (MBSE) artifacts. Structural models are captured with SysML (including internal block diagrams) and component‑connector ADLs such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc and SysML IBDs, defining components, interfaces, and composition. Behavioral aspects are modeled with state‑machine extensions in SysML v1.7 or with MontiArc Automaton, and can be coupled to external simulation tools such as MathWorks Simulink or other domain‑specific simulators. Analytical or code‑level models may be incorporated as C++ or Java implementations. The overall model constellation therefore combines geometric/structural descriptions, behavioral/state‑machine models, and simulation/analytical models, linked to data streams collected from the physical asset via the digital‑shadow one‑way flow.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: An automated one‑way data flow transfers contextual data traces from the physical object to its digital representation. The flow aggregates and abstracts the collected data for a specific purpose, acting as a passive entity without sending data back to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The document mentions that a Digital Twin can provide active components capable of interacting with the physical asset, but it does not describe concrete command types, message formats, validation, authentication, or execution confirmation mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin development follows the MBSE‑driven, multi‑level abstraction lifecycle: (1) Requirements level, capturing stakeholder needs; (2) Functional architecture level, defining functions and interfaces; (3) Logical architecture level, specifying component‑connector structures using ADLs such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc or SysML internal block diagrams; (4) Product/Technical layer, where concrete implementations (e.g., Simulink models, C++/Java code) are realized. Transitions between stages are managed by systematic refinement of models and traceability links, enabling governance of the twin from abstract specifications to executable artifacts.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering of the Digital Twin uses established MBSE methodologies – RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES and SMArDT – to structure the development workflow. Models are created in SysML and refined with architecture description languages (ACME, AADL, MontiArc) and behavioral extensions (MontiArc Automaton, SysML state machines). Simulation and validation are performed with MathWorks Simulink and other simulation tools; code generation targets C++ or Java. While the document does not detail QA or version‑control mechanisms, the methodological stack provides a traceable, model‑centric evolution path for the twin.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The approach aligns with established modeling standards: SysML (an OMG standard) is used for requirements, functional and logical architectures; ADLs such as ACME, AADL and MontiArc are employed for component‑connector specifications; behavioral modeling follows SysML state‑machine semantics and MontiArc Automaton. The digital‑shadow reference model is expressed as a UML class diagram, adhering to UML standards. No other industry‑specific certification or interoperability standards are mentioned.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
